Voters cast their ballots in midterm elections on the 26th, where 24 senators (one-third of the total 72) and 127 representatives (approximately half of the total 257) are elected in Argentina. The right-wing ruling party La Libertad Avanza (LLA) is expected to secure a landslide victory in Argentina’s midterm parliamentary elections held on the 26th. This election served as a \"midterm evaluation\" of the free-market reforms and stringent austerity policies introduced by President Javier Milei’s government, which took office in December 2023. According to local media outlet *Buenos Aires Herald*, with 91% of votes counted nationwide, La Libertad Avanza has recorded a 40.8% vote share. In this election, 24 out of 72 Senate seats and 127 out of 257 House seats are set to be replaced. With this result, La Libertad Avanza is predicted to securely secure one-third of the House seats. This would establish a political foundation to support various policies of the Milei government. Currently, La Libertad Avanza holds 6 seats in the Senate and 40 seats in the House, governing in coalition with parties that share similar political ideologies. U.S. President Donald Trump (left) welcomes Argentine President Javier Milei, who visited the White House on the 14th. The Donald Trump U.S. administration, approximately two weeks before Argentina’s midterm elections on the 9th, approved a currency swap worth 20 billion dollars (approximately 28.5 trillion Korean won) to come to the aid of Milei, who was facing a political crisis. Local media outlets have uniformly reacted with \"an unexpected, surprising victory for the ruling party.\" They analyze that the support from the Trump administration is cited as a key factor in the strong victory. Javier Milei, Argentina's president, arrives to cast a ballot at a polling station during the midterm elections in Buenos Aires, Argentina, on Sunday, October 26, 2025. President Javier Milei has emerged strengthened from Argentina’s legislative elections, with results showing his party may have secured close to 40 percent of the national vote. With 91.5 of polling stations reporting nationwide, Milei’s La Libertad Avanza had 40.8 percent of the national vote cast for national deputies. Preliminary results showed strong performances for La Libertad Avanza in the provinces of Córdoba and Santa Fe. Milei’s party also looked odds-on to notch victories in Buenos Aires City, Tierra del Fuego, Río Negro, Neuquén, Chaco and Salta. In the nation’s closely watched key battleground region, Buenos Aires Province, home to some 40 percent of the electorate, La Libertad Avanza was neck-and-neck with the opposition Fuerza Patria coalition. National Security Minister Patricia Bullrich, running as a LLA candidate for Senate in Buenos Aires City, wrote simply “!!!” on social media. The legislative elections were the first true national test of support for Milei since he won office two years ago on a promise to revive Argentina’s long-ailing economy. The election run-up was marked by a run on the national currency, the peso, that forced Milei to seek a bailout from US President Donald Trump. Argentina’s President Javier Milei secured a decisive victory in the 26 October 2025 midterm elections, winning nearly 41% of the vote and dramatically expanding his party, La Libertad Avanza, in Congress. Despite these controversies, Milei’s increased legislative support now allows him to push forward his Thatcherite-style economic programme, which includes deregulation, privatization, and market liberalization. International observers, including leaders like Giorgia Meloni and Nigel Farage, are watching Milei’s populist economic experiment closely, as Argentina’s approach could influence right-wing populist strategies globally. Crowds of Milei supporters celebrated in Buenos Aires, seeing the victory as a chance to “make Argentina great again.” However, critics highlight the human cost of austerity: factory closures, rising unemployment, and pension cuts. The success of Milei’s reforms hinges on maintaining US support, managing inflation, and balancing austerity with social needs. Milei’s strengthened mandate positions him for further reforms ahead of the 2027 presidential election, but the sustainability of his radical economic agenda remains uncertain. ### Q: Who is Javier Milei and why is he significant in Argentina? A: Javier Milei is Argentina’s president, elected in 2023. A: Milei’s party, La Libertad Avanza, won nearly 41% of the vote, securing 13 Senate seats and 64 lower-house seats, significantly increasing legislative support. ### Q: How has Milei’s austerity policy impacted Argentina? A: His “chainsaw” approach cut public spending on education, pensions, and healthcare, resulting in layoffs, factory closures, and economic contraction, though supporters credit him with reducing inflation. A: Milei faces managing inflation, economic growth, depleted reserves, and public discontent. A: Supporters celebrated his win as a chance for reform, while critics worried about the social impact of austerity, including pensions, healthcare, and employment.
